chunk_summary: Case vignettes illustrate follow-up and referral decisions for moderate acute malnutrition (MAM), focusing on high-risk markers, adherence challenges, TB suspicion, and relapse management within supplementary feeding programs.
learning_objectives: Understand the use of MUAC and WHZ discordance in MAM risk assessment.
Learn strategies to address non-response due to sharing and dilution of therapeutic food.
Recognize signs of TB suspicion in malnourished children and initiate appropriate referrals.
Develop follow-up plans for relapse cases in MAM management.
key_facts: MUAC <125 mm and WHZ <-2 are critical markers for MAM risk classification.
High-risk criteria include age under 24 months and MUAC <121 mm.
Non-response may result from household sharing or improper food preparation.
TB suspicion in malnourished children requires early referral via NTEP diagnostic pathways.
Relapse within 2 months of discharge indicates unresolved drivers and necessitates intensified follow-up.
reference_quote: Guideline on the prevention and management of wasting and nutritional oedema in infants and children under 5 years emphasizes prioritizing high-risk markers like MUAC and WHZ for early intervention.
document_key_topics: Referral pathways for TB suspicion in malnourished children
